story:
An August night lay on us like warm, wet cloth, the backyard flickering with fireflies. My best friend Kenny and my cousins—Toby, Lisa, and Fred—ran in that big yard that met the woods where I wasn’t supposed to go. Mom still brought up the time I came home covered in poison ivy. Last summer Toby stepped on a hornets’ nest; I can still hear him bawling and Aunt Sylvie scolding him for going where he shouldn’t.

Fred and Lisa spun in circles with jelly jars, catching fireflies and calling them “bug lanterns.” They showed them off like trophies. I thought they were a little crazy. Me and Kenny stood at the shadowy line where yard met trees, scheming about mischief that didn’t get us caught. Sometimes bats flickered through the branches while cicadas buzzed their steady song. A faint mist pooled low and pale, strange but somehow familiar, like a half-remembered dream.

Then the night went hollow. The chirring stopped; the fireflies blinked out. The ground shivered under our sneakers. From deep in the woods came a low rumble rolling toward us. Toby, Lisa, and Fred bolted for the back porch where the grown-ups were grilling and laughing over beers. The yard was long; in the haze they looked like they were running through clouds and then they vanished into the white.

Me and Kenny ducked behind a wide pine, pressed flat, then dared to peek. The earth trembled harder. A single giant eye swelled out of the dark, round and blinding, the sound growing until it had no edge. I saw Kenny’s mouth open and his face twist, but I couldn’t hear him. I realized I was screaming too only when, suddenly, I wasn’t in the yard anymore. Kenny and my cousins were circled around me. Their faces were real, close. “He’s awake, Mrs. B! He’s awake!” They shouted for the nurse. Feet rushed. Hands touched me.

I felt as if I had stepped through a door and found both halves of myself meeting in the doorway. Mom and Dad stared at a doctor who bounced his pencil against his desk like it was a metronome. “Your son was in a coma caused by hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy,” he said carefully. “It started with that bee sting on his neck. Lack of oxygen.” My parents sat still as statues, trying to let words become real.

It was midnight when I woke to quiet and a throb of sound far off. Later I learned it was midnight exactly one year from the sting, which was why everyone had been crowded into my room—an anniversary no one wanted but kept anyway.

The rumble came again, but now I was fully awake. I turned my head to the small window. Across the way, lights rose over a train yard. A freighter eased forward, groaning toward the station, headlight pouring a round white eye across the dark. Fear loosened its grip into understanding. I touched the spot on my neck where the bee got me.

On the nightstand sat a jelly jar full of fireflies, my cousins’ “bug lantern,” blinking slow and steady. The train’s noise faded, and the cicadas outside took up their August drone again. I closed my eyes and drifted back to sleep, the light from the jar breathing beside me.
